peer_r1_founder_1.abstract: We propose a unified training-free framework that combines precision allocation and geometric interference cancellation to enhance the robustness and efficiency of inference under distribution shifts. Our approach leverages the spectral properties of activations to dynamically adjust numerical precision while isolating task-relevant subspaces. Specifically, we compute the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states to determine precision requirements and identify interference directions. We then project activations onto a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace to cancel geometric interference, ensuring strict computational budgets. Theoretical bounds link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise and perturbation. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, our framework achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to isolated precision allocation and interference cancellation baselines. This work provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
peer_r1_founder_1.related: Existing work includes Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) for precision allocation and Geometric Signal Separation for interference cancellation. However, these approaches are developed independently and do not exploit their complementary benefits. Our proposal integrates these ideas into a unified framework, leveraging the spectral properties of activations to simultaneously optimize precision allocation and interference cancellation.

peer_r1_founder_2.abstract: We propose Dynamic Geometry Routing (DGR), a training-free framework that leverages the geometry of activation spaces to dynamically adjust inference pathways based on input complexity. By computing the local covariance structure and spectral properties of activations in real-time, DGR identifies inputs that can be processed with reduced computational overhead without sacrificing accuracy. We introduce a routing mechanism that maps geometric diagnostics to compute-saving strategies, such as layer skipping or attention head pruning, while enforcing strict latency budgets. Theoretical bounds link geometric complexity to generalization stability under computational perturbations, providing a formal foundation for the routing mechanism. Experiments on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks demonstrate that DGR achieves superior throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to static and heuristic baselines. Our approach bridges foundational optimization theory with practical, scalable inference, offering a reproducible pathway for resource-efficient adaptation to dynamic input distributions.

peer_r20_founder_2.abstract: Layer-skipping is a promising technique for reducing the computational cost of deep neural network inference. However, existing methods often require costly training or fail to adapt to dynamic input distributions. We propose a training-free layer-skipping framework that uses the Jacobian norm of hidden state transformations as a proxy for layer importance. By computing the Jacobian norm on-the-fly, we dynamically skip layers whose transformations contribute minimally to the final output, ensuring strict latency budgets while preserving model accuracy. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking Jacobian norm thresholds to generalization stability under layer perturbation. Experiments on image classification and natural language processing tasks demonstrate that our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to existing layer-skipping techniques.

By performing on-the-fly singular value decomposition (SVD) of activations, DSA identifies dominant spectral directions and projects computations onto these subspaces, ensuring strict latency budgets while preserving accuracy. We introduce a spectral stability metric derived from the condition number of activation covariances, which serves as a proxy for computational difficulty. Tokens residing in well-conditioned subspaces are routed through fewer layers or lower-precision computations, while ill-conditioned tokens receive full computational resources. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, DSA achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic baselines. Our framework provides a mathematically transparent, reproducible pathway for efficient inference under natural distribution shifts, bridging foundational spectral theory with practical system-scale optimization.

peer_r5_founder_2.abstract: Training-free latency optimization has emerged as a powerful paradigm for resource-efficient inference. However, existing approaches tackle compute-saving mechanisms like mixed precision, layer skipping and attention pruning in isolation, missing opportunities for synergistic optimization. We propose Unified Spectral Compute Throttling (USCT), a framework that coordinates multiple compute-saving mechanisms using a unified spectral diagnostic of activation geometry. At each layer, USCT computes activation covariance statistics that simultaneously inform decisions about numerical precision, layer skipping and attention pruning. These decisions are coordinated to enforce strict wall-clock latency budgets while preserving accuracy, guided by PAC-Bayes generalization bounds. We evaluate USCT on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers compared to isolated compute-saving baselines. By unifying multiple optimization axes under a theoretically grounded framework, USCT provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for practical deployment of resource-efficient inference.
peer_r5_founder_2.related: Existing approaches like Spectral Quantization Routing (founder_1) use activation geometry for mixed-precision allocation, while Spectral Latent Steering (founder_2) focuses on layer skipping. Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation (founder_1) tackles attention pruning. Our proposal distinguishes by introducing a unified diagnostic that evaluates and coordinates all these mechanisms simultaneously, with PAC-Bayes theoretical grounding.

peer_r6_founder_1.abstract: Mixed-precision quantization (MPQ) offers significant computational savings but requires precise layer-wise sensitivity estimation to avoid accuracy degradation. Current methods rely on heuristic proxies or costly training-based search, lacking theoretical grounding and scalability. We propose Curvature-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization (CAMPQ), a training-free framework that leverages the spectral properties of the Hessian matrix to determine optimal bit-width configurations. By computing the Hessian's condition number and eigenvalue decay via randomized SVD, we derive a sensitivity score for each layer that predicts its robustness to quantization. These scores are used to formulate bit-width allocation as a constrained optimization problem, minimizing quantization error under a resource budget. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across vision and language benchmarks, CAMPQ achieves superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art training-free and training-based MPQ methods. Our approach bridges optimization theory with practical deployment, offering a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference.
peer_r6_founder_1.related: Existing mixed-precision quantization methods often rely on heuristic proxies or costly training-based search strategies, as seen in EMQ (Dong et al., 2023) and InfoQ (Akbulut et al., 2025). While these approaches improve efficiency, they lack a principled connection to the underlying optimization landscape. Our proposal leverages the Hessian's spectral properties—specifically, its condition number and eigenvalue decay—to directly quantify quantization sensitivity. This distinguishes our work by grounding precision allocation in formal optimization theory, avoiding the fragility of heuristic proxies and the computational overhead of training-based methods.

peer_r6_founder_2.abstract: We propose a novel framework for latency-aware inference optimization that dynamically routes tokens through low-rank subspaces based on their spectral stability. By computing real-time spectral diagnostics using randomized SVD, we map tokens to subspaces that enforce strict latency budgets while maintaining inference accuracy. Our approach is grounded in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral stability to generalization stability under perturbation. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to existing baselines. This work bridges foundational optimization theory with practical, scalable inference, offering a reproducible, zero-training path to efficient latency-aware routing.
peer_r6_founder_2.related: Existing work on training-free inference optimization, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation, focuses on precision allocation or noise filtering but does not explicitly address latency-aware routing through low-rank subspaces. peer_r7_founder_1.abstract: Fine-tuning large models remains computationally expensive, often requiring full-gradient computations across all layers. We propose Curvature-Aware Gradient Routing (CAGR), a framework that dynamically routes gradients based on the local curvature of the loss landscape. By estimating the Hessian spectral decay using randomized SVD, CAGR identifies subspaces where gradients contribute minimally to optimization progress and prunes them, enforcing strict computational budgets. We theoretically ground CAGR in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking curvature diagnostics to generalization stability under gradient pruning. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAGR achieves comparable performance to full fine-tuning while reducing gradient computation overhead by over 50%. By replacing heuristic pruning strategies with formalized curvature-aware routing, CAGR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient fine-tuning.

This proposal extends these ideas to fine-tuning by leveraging geometric diagnostics of the loss landscape. peer_r7_founder_2.abstract: We propose a novel latency-aware inference framework that leverages the Jacobian matrix to dynamically skip layers with minimal impact on model output. By computing the Jacobian of each layer's output with respect to its input, we identify layers that contribute least to the final prediction and skip them during inference. This method is training-free and enforces strict latency budgets by dynamically adjusting the number of active layers based on the input's characteristics. We evaluate our approach on single-GPU setups across various benchmarks, demonstrating significant throughput improvements while maintaining competitive accuracy. Our framework provides a reproducible, mathematically transparent pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
peer_r7_founder_2.related: Existing approaches to efficient inference often rely on static layer skipping or require costly calibration (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing). Our work distinguishes itself by leveraging the Jacobian matrix to dynamically skip layers based on their contribution to the output, ensuring minimal perturbation while enforcing latency constraints. This approach is training-free and integrates seamlessly into existing architectures.

peer_r8_founder_1.abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant latency and memory gains but typically relies on static profiling or costly calibration, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions. We propose Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity. Tokens residing in well-conditioned, rapidly decaying subspaces are robust to low-precision computation, while ill-conditioned tokens require higher precision. SQR computes these geometric diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SQR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, SQR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.

Our proposal is distinct in leveraging real-time spectral diagnostics of activation covariances to dynamically route tokens to appropriate precision tiers, providing a mathematically transparent and deterministic framework for precision allocation.

peer_r8_founder_2.abstract: We propose Latency-Aware Spectral Routing (LASR), a training-free framework that dynamically routes computations in neural networks based on the spectral properties of activation subspaces. By performing a single-pass PCA on initial layer activations, LASR identifies dominant spectral directions and projects subsequent computations onto this subspace, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground LASR in information-theoretic bounds, showing that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement. Empirically, we demonstrate that LASR matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead by over 70% and enforcing deterministic latency bounds. Our approach eliminates heuristic stagnation detection, replacing it with interpretable spectral diagnostics that yield transparent decision boundaries. LASR bridges the gap between foundational optimization theory and practical, scalable inference, offering a reproducible, zero-training path to efficient test-time scaling.
peer_r8_founder_2.related: Existing work on latency-aware inference often relies on static profiling or heuristic methods, which fail to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Spectral Latent Steering (founder_2) and Spectral Quantization Routing (founder_1) have explored spectral properties for optimization, but they do not explicitly address latency-aware routing. Our proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a training-free framework that dynamically routes computations based on spectral diagnostics, ensuring strict latency bounds.

peer_r9_founder_2.abstract: Latency-aware inference is critical for deploying large models in resource-constrained environments. While layer skipping offers a promising solution, existing methods often rely on heuristic or static strategies, limiting their adaptability. We hypothesize that the spectral decay rate of hidden state activations serves as a robust proxy for layer redundancy, enabling training-free, latency-aware layer skipping. We propose Spectral Decay-Driven Layer Skipping (SDLS), a framework that computes the spectral decay rate of hidden states on-the-fly using randomized SVD and skips layers when the decay exceeds a calibrated threshold. We ground SDLS in PAC-Bayes theory, linking spectral decay to generalization stability under layer omission. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across language modeling and vision benchmarks, SDLS achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to heuristic layer skipping baselines. By replacing qualitative redundancy claims with formalized spectral diagnostics, SDLS provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for efficient inference.

Our proposal uniquely leverages spectral decay to dynamically skip layers, offering a mathematically grounded and computationally frugal alternative.

Current methods often rely on heuristic proxies or computationally expensive search algorithms, limiting their scalability and interpretability. We propose a novel training-free approach that leverages spectral decomposition of weight matrices to guide bit-width allocation. By identifying the principal components of each layer, we can allocate higher precision to the most critical weights while aggressively quantizing less important ones. This approach provides a mathematically transparent and deterministic framework for MPQ, ensuring numerical stability and efficient resource utilization. We validate our method on a range of vision and language models, demonstrating significant improvements in accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art methods.

DYNAMO dynamically quantifies layer sensitivity using Jacobian matrices and prunes tokens based on their contribution to the final output, enabling adaptive inference without additional training. Experiments on ViTs, LLMs, and VLMs demonstrate that DYNAMO achieves up to 2.5× speedup with minimal accuracy loss, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. Our framework offers a generalizable solution for efficient inference in transformer-based models.

We evaluate JGLS on multiple transformer architectures (e.g., GPT, BERT, ViT) across diverse tasks, demonstrating significant reductions in inference latency (up to 40%) with minimal accuracy degradation (<1%). random_r16_founder_2.abstract: Transformer models have achieved remarkable success across various tasks, but their computational demands remain a significant bottleneck, particularly for real-time applications. Our approach introduces a lightweight Jacobian computation mechanism that operates at inference time, allowing us to identify and skip layers that contribute minimally to the final output. We validate our approach on both vision and language transformers, demonstrating significant reductions in inference latency with minimal accuracy loss. Our results show that JLS achieves a 2.5x speedup on ViT-H/14 and a 1.8x speedup on GPT-3, outperforming existing layer skipping methods by a margin of 0.5x in latency reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy.

random_r8_founder_2.abstract: Inference optimization is crucial for deploying neural networks in resource-constrained environments. This paper introduces a novel training-free approach to inference optimization by leveraging the sparsity of Jacobian matrices in neural networks. We propose a Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping (JBLS) framework that selectively skips layers during inference based on the sparsity of their Jacobian matrices. By analyzing the Jacobian sparsity, JBLS identifies layers that contribute minimally to the output, allowing them to be skipped without significant loss in model accuracy. We validate our approach on several benchmark datasets and architectures, demonstrating that JBLS can reduce computational overhead by up to 40% while maintaining model performance. Our results highlight the potential of Jacobian sparsity as a key factor in training-free inference optimization.

Recent work on Jacobian sparsity in neural networks (e.g., Matrix-Free Second-Order Optimization of Gaussian Splats) has shown that Jacobian matrices exhibit significant sparsity, but this has not been systematically exploited for training-free inference optimization. Our proposal distinguishes itself by directly leveraging Jacobian sparsity to inform layer skipping decisions, without requiring additional training or heuristic tuning.

random_r9_founder_1.abstract: Mixed-precision quantization (MPQ) offers a promising trade-off between model efficiency and accuracy but often relies on heuristic proxies or calibration data, limiting its theoretical transparency and generalizability. This paper introduces a training-free framework for MPQ based on spectral curvature diagnostics. By analyzing the Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance of weight matrices, we derive deterministic proxies for precision allocation that quantify the sensitivity of each layer to quantization error. Our approach replaces heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii and explicit condition number constraints, ensuring numerical stability and architectural coherence. Experiments on ResNet and Vision Transformer architectures demonstrate that our framework achieves competitive accuracy-complexity trade-offs while maintaining strict computational overhead bounds. This work bridges foundational optimization theory with practical MPQ deployment, offering a resource-efficient and mathematically transparent solution for single-GPU validation.

This proposal distinguishes itself by formalizing precision allocation through spectral curvature analysis, providing a mathematically transparent and deterministic framework.

random_r9_founder_2.abstract: Transformer models have revolutionized machine learning but face significant inference latency challenges. While token pruning, layer skipping, and quantization individually offer efficiency gains, their static application limits their effectiveness. This paper introduces DynaAccel, a dynamic, context-aware inference acceleration framework that adaptively combines these techniques based on input context and intermediate activations. DynaAccel employs a lightweight controller trained via reinforcement learning to decide the optimal combination of acceleration strategies at each step, balancing latency and accuracy. Experiments on vision transformers and language models demonstrate that DynaAccel achieves up to 3x inference speedup with less than 1% accuracy loss, outperforming state-of-the-art static acceleration methods.

This proposal uniquely integrates these approaches dynamically, leveraging input context and activation patterns to adaptively apply the most effective acceleration strategy at each inference step.
